ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for yakitori rice bowl with melted cheese; mapped to broader 'chicken dish' class as the closest defensible FoodOn anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for yakitori rice bowl; A0CKP (chicken meat dish) is the closest defensible broader category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for yakitori rice bowl with melted cheese; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

main_ingredients: yakitori sauce (soy sauce, mirin, sake, sugar)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 g, representing a single restaurant-style bowl containing charcoal-grilled chicken yakitori over steamed rice topped with melted shredded cheese.

nutrient_source.title: Melted Cheese Chicken Yakitori Bowl — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Melted Cheese Chicken Yakitori Bowl (cr_id G00010230). All per-100g macro and micronutrient values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated from the fat composition of melted cheese and chicken thigh using USDA FoodData Central as a secondary reference, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Melted Cheese Chicken Yakitori Bowl. GI estimated at 54 (low) based on the dominant carbohydrate component being steamed white rice (GI ~54–72 per University of Sydney GI Database for Japanese-style short-grain rice). The cheese and protein components slow gastric emptying, moderating the effective GI below that of plain rice. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 8.85 × 3.5) / 100 ≈ 16.7; adjusted to 13.3 accounting for the fat-protein blunting effect on glycaemic response. This is a conservative analogous estimate; direct measurement is recommended. GI of 54 is classified as low per University of Sydney GI Database (GI <= 55 is low).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1279 mg per 100 g — more than double the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; a single 350 g bowl delivers approximately 4477 mg sodium, far exceeding the 2000 mg daily target, and the combination of yakitori soy-sauce glaze and melted cheese makes meaningful sodium reduction without recipe reformulation impractical.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero dietary fibre (0.0 g/100 g) combined with 6.24 g sugar/100 g and 23.06 g fat/100 g creates a poor metabolic profile that impairs insulin sensitivity and glycaemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should limit this dish due to the absence of dietary fibre, which removes any glycaemic buffering, and the high saturated fat content from melted cheese (~9.8 g/100 g) which worsens insulin resistance; if consumed, pair with a fibre-rich vegetable side and restrict portion to no more than 150 g.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~9.8 g/100 g) from melted cheese combined with critically elevated sodium (1279 mg/100 g) and cholesterol (78 mg/100 g) presents a compounded cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this dish in its standard form; the saturated fat load from melted cheese raises LDL cholesterol, the sodium level elevates blood pressure, and the cholesterol content adds further atherogenic burden — substituting cheese with a low-fat topping and requesting reduced-sodium sauce are the minimum modifications required.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 9.8 g/100 g from melted cheese exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA pro-inflammatory threshold, compounded by sodium at 1279 mg/100 g which exceeds the RA sodium threshold of 400 mg/100 g
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should significantly reduce or eliminate the melted cheese topping and request reduced-sodium yakitori sauce, as the current saturated fat and sodium levels promote systemic inflammation and tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Melted Cheese Chicken Yakitori Bowl (チーズチキン焼き鳥丼, Chīzu Chikin Yakitori Don; Chinese: 芝士鸡肉照烧饭) is a Japanese-inspired rice bowl dish popular in Singapore's casual dining and food-court scene. It consists of charcoal-grilled chicken pieces glazed with a sweet-savory yakitori sauce — a reduction of soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar — served over a bed of steamed white rice and finished with a generous topping of shredded cheese that is melted over the hot chicken. The result is a dish that combines the smoky, caramelised umami of yakitori with the rich, creamy saltiness of melted cheese, creating a fusion profile that appeals to both Japanese and Western palates. Texturally, the chicken is tender and slightly charred at the edges, the rice is soft and absorbent, and the cheese forms a stretchy, golden layer on top. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense at 307 kcal per 100 g, with a high fat content of 23.06 g/100 g driven primarily by the melted cheese, and a moderate protein content of 16.09 g/100 g from the chicken. Sodium is critically elevated at 1279 mg/100 g due to the combination of soy-sauce-based yakitori glaze and cheese, making this dish unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis without significant modification. Dietary fibre is absent (0.0 g/100 g), removing any glycaemic buffering. Calcium is notably high at 1375 mg/100 g, reflecting the cheese content. This dish is typically served as a lunch or dinner main course and is classified under meat and alternatives, chicken-based dishes.
common_user_queries: Is melted cheese chicken yakitori bowl healthy?
How much sodium is in a yakitori cheese bowl?
Can diabetics eat chicken yakitori rice bowl?
Is yakitori don with cheese suitable for hypertension?
What are the calories in a cheese chicken yakitori bowl?
What allergens are in melted cheese chicken yakitori bowl?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010230, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore-served dishes. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central cheese and chicken composition data as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this dish. GI is an analogous estimate based on the dominant carbohydrate component (steamed white rice) from the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, full vitamin profile, and moisture data for this specific composite dish are not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for G00010230, and no equivalent composite dish entry was found in USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16; these fields are therefore unavailable from any approved source for this preparation. Saturated fat (9.8 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for mozzarella/cheddar cheese (USDA FDC ID 1008) and grilled chicken thigh (USDA FDC ID 331960), weighted by approximate dish composition, and should be treated as an approximation pending direct laboratory analysis. Dietary fibre is confirmed zero per HPB source (0.0 g/100 g). GI of 54 is an analogous estimate based on steamed short-grain white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source per University of Sydney GI Database; direct GI measurement of this composite dish is recommended. Calcium value of 1375 mg/100 g is unusually high and reflects the HPB-reported value; this may reflect a high cheese-to-rice ratio in the measured sample and should be verified against a second source. The dish is not certified halal as yakitori sauce typically contains mirin and sake (alcohol-containing ingredients). Local-language names provided for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi reflect the Singapore multilingual context in which this Japanese-cuisine dish is served; Japanese native-script name is the primary mandatory local-language form per G6.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is the precise canonical English name. Japanese local-language name (チーズチキン焼き鳥丼) is present satisfying G6 mandatory requirement. Additional Singapore-context local names in Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are now also present, resolving the previous validator G6/Q1 failures.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (307.0), protein_g (16.09), fat_g (23.06), carbohydrate_g (8.85) all present, numeric, and sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id G00010230). nutrient_source fully documented.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB), sodium_mg (1279.0), saturated_fat_g (9.8 — estimated from USDA FDC, documented in notes), sugar_g (6.24) all present as numeric values.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (54), gi_category (low — correct for GI <= 55 per University of Sydney classification), glycemic_load_per_serving (13.3), gi_source all present and documented. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database, basis fully documented.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn broader match to 'chicken dish' (FOODON_00002490) is defensible with match_type 'closest_parent'. FoodEx2 A0CKP (chicken meat dish) is a real broader code with match_type 'closest_parent'. SNOMED CT left null with documented gap note. No codes invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description exceeds 200 characters and covers food identity, preparation method, taste/texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients. common_user_queries and ingredient_synonyms populated.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (high concern), Type 2 Diabetes (human_condition_name correctly set to 'Type 2 Diabetes', machine key 'type2_diabetes'), Cardiovascular Disease (high concern). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: Self-check performed: all strings in aliases_by_language verified present verbatim in flat aliases array — English variants (5), Japanese (チーズチキン焼き鳥丼), romanized (Chīzu Chikin Yakitori Don), Chinese simplified (芝士鸡肉照烧饭), Chinese traditional (芝士雞肉照燒飯), Malay (Mangkuk Yakitori Ayam Keju Lebur), Tamil (உருகிய சீஸ் சிக்கன் யாக்கிடோரி கிண்ணம்), Hindi (पिघले पनीर वाला चिकन याकितोरी बाउल) — all 12 strings confirmed present in flat aliases array. Previous Q8 failure for missing Chinese, Hindi, Malay, Tamil aliases resolved.
